articles.title: Local Git Remotes
articles.summary: Local git remotes allow developers to easily manage multiple repositories from a single location, such as a home server or a cloud storage service. By setting up a local remote repository using a bare clone and associating it with a specific branch, developers can push and pull changes without worrying about conflicts or downtime. This setup also enables the use of SSH authentication for secure access to the remote repository, making it more reliable than traditional HTTP-based connections.

articles.title: Leaving performance on the table

This approach allows for more accurate optimization than instrumented binaries that rely solely on compiler flags.

articles.title: You probably don't need Yocto, and that's fine
articles.summary: Yocto is often considered a default choice for embedded Linux projects due to its flexibility and vendor support, but it can also be a trap when developers don't need that much control. A regular Yocto release typically lasts only seven months before being updated, while Long Term Support (LTS) releases provide up to four years of updates. articles.title: SQLite Does Not Accept Agentic Code
articles.summary: SQLite has introduced a new policy that prohibits accepting agentic code, meaning any changes made by external developers or agents without prior agreement and/or accompanying legal paperwork. This change is intended to ensure the integrity of the SQLite codebase, which is not intended for development purposes but rather for use in pointing agents at it. The project will accept agentic bug reports that include a reproducible test case, while patches or pull requests demonstrating a possible fix are also welcome.
